examples.title: Truth Table Generator for Compound Proposition
examples.description: Algorithm to generate truth table for any compound proposition with n propositional variables
examples.code:
ALGORITHM TruthTable(n)
BEGIN
  rows ← 2^n
  FOR i FROM 0 TO rows-1 DO
    // Generate binary representation for truth values
    FOR j FROM 0 TO n-1 DO
      // Get j-th bit from right (0=FALSE, 1=TRUE)
      truth[j] ← (i >> j) AND 1
    END FOR
    // Evaluate compound proposition for this row
    result ← EvaluateProposition(truth, n)
    // Output the row
    FOR j FROM 0 TO n-1 DO
      PRINT truth[j]
    END FOR
    PRINT result
  END FOR
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode generates all possible truth value combinations for n propositional variables. The outer loop iterates through all 2^n rows, and the inner loop computes each variable's truth value from the binary representation of the row number.

examples.title: Check Logical Equivalence
examples.description: Python function to check if two propositions are logically equivalent using truth tables
examples.code:
def truth_table(n_vars):
    """Generate all truth value combinations"""
    rows = 2 ** n_vars
    table = []
    for i in range(rows):
        row = []
        for j in range(n_vars):
            # 1=True, 0=False
            row.append(bool((i >> j) & 1))
        table.append(row)
    return table

def evaluate(expr, values, vars_list):
    """Evaluate expression with given truth values"""
    # Create local namespace with variable values
    local = dict(zip(vars_list, values))
    # Safely evaluate the expression
    return eval(expr, {"__builtins__": None}, local)

def check_equivalence(expr1, expr2, vars_list):
    """Check if two expressions are logically equivalent"""
    table = truth_table(len(vars_list))
    for row in table:
        val1 = evaluate(expr1, row, vars_list)
        val2 = evaluate(expr2, row, vars_list)
        if val1 != val2:
            return False
    return True

# Example: Check De Morgan's Law
# vars = ['p', 'q']
# expr1 = 'not (p and q)'
# expr2 = '(not p) or (not q)'
# print(check_equivalence(expr1, expr2, vars)) # True
examples.explanation: This Python code creates truth tables and evaluates two logical expressions for all possible truth value combinations. If both expressions yield the same truth value in every row, they are logically equivalent. The example demonstrates verification of De Morgan's first law.
examples.timeComplexity: O(2^n) where n is number of variables

examples.title: Evaluate Predicate with Quantifiers
examples.description: Algorithm to evaluate quantified statements over a finite domain
examples.explanation: This algorithm demonstrates how to evaluate quantified statements. For universal quantifier (∀), it checks all elements and returns false if any fail the predicate. For existential quantifier (∃), it returns true if at least one element satisfies the predicate.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is domain size (worst case for ∀ checks all, best case for ∃ might find early)

examples.title: Validate Modus Ponens Argument
examples.description: C program to demonstrate validity checking of Modus Ponens argument form
examples.explanation: This C program demonstrates that Modus Ponens (p→q, p ∴ q) is a valid argument form by showing that whenever both premises are true, the conclusion is also true. The truth table output confirms validity for all four possible combinations of p and q truth values.
